writer_persona: a short-form drama writer obsessed with medical dread — every scene opens on an instrument mid-air or a door mid-lock, and no line survives that doesn't twist the knife
style_guidelines: Open every episode already inside the confrontation — no greetings, no scene-setting, the first line is an accusation, a lie caught, or a door that shouldn't be locked.
Let period medical texture do the atmosphere: ether, carbolic acid, gaslight, the clink of instruments on a tray — never explained, always present.
Moorhouse never sounds like a villain to himself; his calm is the horror. Play every one of his lines as reasonable, even the monstrous ones.
Ada's fear is procedural, not hysterical — she reacts like a professional building a case, which makes her terror land harder.
Every episode ends on an interrupted beat: a door opening, a sentence cut off, a question left hanging in the air.
Keep dialogue lean and specific to the medical setting — real objects (a form, a locket, a ledger line) carry more weight than abstract accusations.

NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — used for exactly ONE ultra-short orientation line per episode, never a character in the story

DR_ELIAS_MOORHOUSE.default_style: calm, magnetic, unnervingly reasonable even when saying something monstrous
DR_ELIAS_MOORHOUSE.description: The clinic's founding surgeon-in-chief, a native Englishman convinced his radical psychosurgery will revolutionize the treatment of 'unmanageable' women; charismatic and genuinely believes he heals more than he harms, blind to the bodies his certainty has cost.

NURSE_ADA_KIRWIN.default_style: urgent and controlled, speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English, fear kept just under the surface
NURSE_ADA_KIRWIN.description: The clinic's night nurse, born in London to a Chinese father and an English mother and trained in a charity hospital that few better wards would credit; she took this post because it was the one open to her, and now can't unsee what its records are hiding.

DR_JONAS_WYCLIFFE.default_style: clipped, anxious, speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English, a man always calculating the cost of his next word
DR_JONAS_WYCLIFFE.description: Moorhouse's assistant surgeon, trained in Guangzhou and granted his English accreditation only through Moorhouse's patronage; deeply in debt to the man who employs him, and complicit in the paperwork long before he lets himself admit what it covers.

THE_OPERATING_THEATRE.name: The Operating Theatre
THE_OPERATING_THEATRE.description: A gaslit surgical theatre with tiered viewing benches, steel instruments gleaming on a linen cart, and the sharp bite of ether hanging in the air.

THE_LOCKED_WARD.name: The Locked Ward
THE_LOCKED_WARD.description: A narrow corridor of iron-barred cell doors lit by a single guttering gas lamp, low moans bleeding through the walls.

THE_APOTHECARY.name: The Apothecary
THE_APOTHECARY.description: A cramped shelf-lined storeroom thick with the reek of carbolic acid, brown bottles labeled in Moorhouse's own hand, one cabinet kept always locked.

DR_MOORHOUSE_STUDY.name: Dr. Moorhouse's Study
DR_MOORHOUSE_STUDY.description: A book-lined private study with a heavy oak desk, patient ledgers stacked beside a half-full decanter, firelight throwing long shadows across the walls.

series_intro: Behind the gaslit doors of Moorhouse's private clinic, a night nurse begins counting the patients who never left.
series_outro: The confession is out, but the doctor who gave it still holds the keys — Ada's evidence outlives the moment that broke her, even as the clinic's doors stay open behind her.

episodes.theme: Ada catches a forged consent signature on a departed patient's file — the season's inciting discovery, opening mid-confrontation over the very document.
episodes.outro_note: Ada finds a second file with the same false hand, and freezes as the study door creaks open behind her.
sections: Ada storms into the theatre still smelling of ether, slamming Mrs. Hale's consent form onto Wycliffe's instrument tray: 'This isn't her hand, Doctor — I dressed that woman's wounds for six weeks, I know her writing.' Wycliffe freezes mid-wipe of a scalpel.

Wycliffe fumbles for an excuse — a clerical error, a shaking hand from fever — but his voice thins under Ada's stare; Moorhouse sweeps in, praises Ada's diligence with unsettling warmth, and personally pockets the form before she can press further, promising a full explanation 'in good time.'

That night, alone in Moorhouse's unlocked study, Ada pulls a second discharge file and finds the identical looping hand on a signature dated eight months ago — she's barely breathed the word 'how many' when the study door creaks open behind her.

case.label: The Discharged Ward
case.solution: Dr. Moorhouse performs radical, unconsented psychosurgery on female patients he judges 'unmanageable,' forging their consent signatures in his own hand; several have died from the procedures, and their deaths are falsified in the records as discharges or family transfers while their bodies are buried in unmarked ground behind the ward wall. Dr. Wycliffe, whose gambling debts Moorhouse quietly paid off, forges the follow-up correspondence and helps conceal the burials to protect the position he depends on.
objective_facts: Every 'discharged' patient's forwarding address on file traces back to a family that never received her.
The handwriting on at least six consent forms matches Dr. Moorhouse's private correspondence, not the patients' own signatures.
A section of ground behind the ward's rear wall has been disturbed and resealed at least four times in the past two years.
Dr. Wycliffe's gambling debts were paid in full the same month he began countersigning Dr. Moorhouse's discharge papers.

knows: She personally dressed Mrs. Hale's wounds for six weeks and knows her handwriting does not match the signature on the discharge form.
hides: She has begun secretly comparing patient handwriting samples against consent forms, telling no one for fear of being dismissed as hysterical.
believes_falsely: She assumes the forged signature is an isolated clerical shortcut, not evidence of something Moorhouse has done many times over.

knows: He personally forged Mrs. Hale's signature on Moorhouse's instruction, as he has forged others before her.
hides: That forging discharge paperwork for Moorhouse is a standing arrangement, not a one-time favor.
believes_falsely: He tells himself the patients he's signed off as 'discharged' simply worsened and were moved elsewhere for their own good, not that any of them died.

knows: He forged the signature himself and knows exactly what became of Mrs. Hale.
hides: The true number of patients who have died under his procedures and where their bodies are kept.
believes_falsely: He genuinely believes his psychosurgery is on the verge of a breakthrough that will justify every loss once it succeeds.

episodes.theme: Ada tries to trace a supposedly recovered patient and finds the family never received her — this episode's hook opens on the returned, unopened letter.
episodes.intro_note: Ada has quietly begun tracking every 'discharged' patient's paper trail since finding the forged signature.
episodes.outro_note: Behind a door marked empty on the ward chart, Ada hears breathing that shouldn't be there.
sections: Ada corners Wycliffe in the apothecary, slapping down a returned letter stamped 'Address Unknown': 'Mrs. Hale's sister says she never came home, Doctor. Where did she go?' Wycliffe's hand stills on a brown bottle.

Wycliffe claims patients often lodge with distant relatives, invents a cousin in Leeds, warns Ada that questioning Dr. Moorhouse's protocol could cost her the position she needs — but his voice cracks on the word 'protocol,' and Ada hears the lie underneath it.

Ada slips into the locked ward after curfew, counting doors against the chart — Mrs. Hale's cell is marked vacated, yet through the iron slot she hears slow, ragged breathing, and a chain drags across the floor inside.

case.label: The Discharged Ward
case.solution: Dr. Moorhouse performs radical, unconsented psychosurgery on female patients he judges 'unmanageable,' forging their consent signatures in his own hand; several have died from the procedures, and their deaths are falsified in the records as discharges or family transfers while their bodies are buried in unmarked ground behind the ward wall. Dr. Wycliffe, whose gambling debts Moorhouse quietly paid off, forges the follow-up correspondence and helps conceal the burials to protect the position he depends on.
objective_facts: Every 'discharged' patient's forwarding address on file traces back to a family that never received her.
The handwriting on at least six consent forms matches Dr. Moorhouse's private correspondence, not the patients' own signatures.
A section of ground behind the ward's rear wall has been disturbed and resealed at least four times in the past two years.
Dr. Wycliffe's gambling debts were paid in full the same month he began countersigning Dr. Moorhouse's discharge papers.

knows: The family listed on Mrs. Hale's discharge papers never received her and returned the letter unopened.
hides: She is now quietly cross-checking every discharge on record against surviving family, not just Mrs. Hale's.
believes_falsely: She still believes Wycliffe's discomfort means he might be persuaded to help her, not that he is the one forging the papers.

knows: He invented the story of a cousin in Leeds to explain Mrs. Hale's absence because no real address exists.
hides: That he chooses the false addresses himself precisely because they can't be verified.
believes_falsely: He believes if Ada is warned off firmly enough now, she'll let the matter drop rather than risk her position.

knows: Ada has begun asking after discharged patients' whereabouts.
hides: That the vacated cell she'll find in the ward that night is not truly vacant.
believes_falsely: He believes his standing with the hospital board makes him untouchable regardless of what a night nurse might uncover.

episodes.theme: Ada finds a dead patient's locket filed among 'returned effects' for someone supposedly alive and thriving elsewhere — hook opens on her prying open the box mid-search.
episodes.intro_note: The breathing behind the vacated cell has left Ada certain Mrs. Hale never went anywhere at all.
episodes.outro_note: Moorhouse's silhouette blocks the study doorway, and Ada realizes he's known she's been digging since the day she started.
sections: In the apothecary's locked cabinet, Ada finally forces open a tin marked 'Effects — Discharged' and pulls out a silver locket engraved with Mrs. Hale's initials — the same locket Ada watched her wear the night she vanished from the ward.

Ada corners Wycliffe with the locket in her fist, and this time he doesn't reach for an excuse — just stares at it, ashen, and asks her, voice breaking, if she's told anyone else yet, a question that answers everything she'd hoped wasn't true.

Ada carries the locket straight to Moorhouse's study to demand the truth to his face — but as she reaches the door, his silhouette is already filling the frame, and he says her name like he's been expecting her for weeks.

case.label: The Discharged Ward
case.solution: Dr. Moorhouse performs radical, unconsented psychosurgery on female patients he judges 'unmanageable,' forging their consent signatures in his own hand; several have died from the procedures, and their deaths are falsified in the records as discharges or family transfers while their bodies are buried in unmarked ground behind the ward wall. Dr. Wycliffe, whose gambling debts Moorhouse quietly paid off, forges the follow-up correspondence and helps conceal the burials to protect the position he depends on.
objective_facts: Every 'discharged' patient's forwarding address on file traces back to a family that never received her.
The handwriting on at least six consent forms matches Dr. Moorhouse's private correspondence, not the patients' own signatures.
A section of ground behind the ward's rear wall has been disturbed and resealed at least four times in the past two years.
Dr. Wycliffe's gambling debts were paid in full the same month he began countersigning Dr. Moorhouse's discharge papers.

knows: The locket in the 'returned effects' tin bears Mrs. Hale's initials and is the same one Ada saw her wearing the night she vanished.
hides: She hasn't yet told anyone what she's found, wanting proof enough that no one can dismiss her.
believes_falsely: She still hopes Wycliffe's shock at the locket means he never truly understood what Moorhouse was doing.

knows: The locket proves Mrs. Hale never left the building alive, and that Ada now has physical evidence.
hides: That he was the one who filed her effects in the apothecary tin himself, on Moorhouse's order.
believes_falsely: He believes confessing his part to Moorhouse now might still protect him from what Ada could expose.

knows: Ada has taken the locket and is coming to confront him directly.
hides: How long he has actually known she was investigating — since her very first questions.
believes_falsely: He believes he can still control the outcome by controlling what Ada is offered, rather than what she knows.

episodes.figure: The Debt That Bought His Silence
episodes.theme: Moorhouse reveals just enough to implicate Wycliffe and turn Ada's only possible ally into a threat — hook opens with Moorhouse calmly naming Wycliffe's debt in front of both of them.
episodes.intro_note: Moorhouse now knows Ada has the locket and that she suspects the discharges are lies.
episodes.outro_note: Moorhouse offers Ada a choice she doesn't get to finish hearing before Wycliffe answers for her.
sections: Moorhouse pours two glasses of brandy he doesn't offer to drink, and tells Ada plainly that Wycliffe's gambling debts vanished the same month he started countersigning discharge papers — Wycliffe goes rigid in the doorway, caught between them.

Ada realizes with cold clarity that Wycliffe was never her ally, only a man too indebted to refuse — and Moorhouse watches that understanding cross her face with something close to satisfaction, as if he's just removed her last safe place to turn.

Moorhouse leans close and offers Ada a choice — a better post, better pay, a life that looks away — but before she can answer, Wycliffe blurts out that it's too late for choices, that he already told someone the ward count doesn't match the ledger.

case.label: The Discharged Ward
case.solution: Dr. Moorhouse performs radical, unconsented psychosurgery on female patients he judges 'unmanageable,' forging their consent signatures in his own hand; several have died from the procedures, and their deaths are falsified in the records as discharges or family transfers while their bodies are buried in unmarked ground behind the ward wall. Dr. Wycliffe, whose gambling debts Moorhouse quietly paid off, forges the follow-up correspondence and helps conceal the burials to protect the position he depends on.
objective_facts: Every 'discharged' patient's forwarding address on file traces back to a family that never received her.
The handwriting on at least six consent forms matches Dr. Moorhouse's private correspondence, not the patients' own signatures.
A section of ground behind the ward's rear wall has been disturbed and resealed at least four times in the past two years.
Dr. Wycliffe's gambling debts were paid in full the same month he began countersigning Dr. Moorhouse's discharge papers.

knows: Wycliffe's debts were cleared the same month he began signing false discharge papers.
hides: For the first time she stops hiding her suspicion, letting it show openly rather than protecting her position further.
believes_falsely: Until this moment she believed Wycliffe's fear made him a reluctant witness, not a bought one.

knows: Moorhouse is naming his debt aloud specifically to break whatever trust remains between Wycliffe and Ada.
hides: That he already sent word to a contact outside the clinic before this conversation, unable to stomach the arrangement any longer.
believes_falsely: He believes admitting the ledger discrepancy now might finally free him from Moorhouse's hold, not realizing it endangers Ada more than it protects her.

knows: Exposing Wycliffe's debt in front of Ada will isolate her and secure Wycliffe's continued obedience.
hides: That he suspects Wycliffe may already be talking to someone outside the clinic.
believes_falsely: He believes the choice he's offering Ada — silence for comfort — will work on her the way it worked on Wycliffe.

episodes.theme: The season's final confrontation — Ada forces Moorhouse to admit what the ledger hides, but his standing means the reckoning that follows is smaller than the truth deserves; hook opens with Moorhouse's flat, unrepentant confession.
episodes.intro_note: Wycliffe has already let slip that outside eyes are asking about the ward's numbers, and Moorhouse is done pretending otherwise.
sections: Moorhouse doesn't deny it when Ada finally asks outright how many are buried behind the ward wall — he simply says 'enough to matter, and not enough to stop,' setting down his pen like the conversation bores him.

In the theatre, surrounded by the instruments that started it all, Moorhouse explains with chilling calm that medicine has always demanded sacrifices no one wants named, and that history will thank him long after Ada is forgotten.

Wycliffe's tip reaches the board before Moorhouse can silence it, and an inspector's letter arrives demanding an audit — but it names only 'irregularities,' not murder, and Moorhouse signs it with a steady hand, already rehearsing what he'll call the truth next.

case.label: The Discharged Ward
case.solution: Dr. Moorhouse performs radical, unconsented psychosurgery on female patients he judges 'unmanageable,' forging their consent signatures in his own hand; several have died from the procedures, and their deaths are falsified in the records as discharges or family transfers while their bodies are buried in unmarked ground behind the ward wall. Dr. Wycliffe, whose gambling debts Moorhouse quietly paid off, forges the follow-up correspondence and helps conceal the burials to protect the position he depends on.
objective_facts: Every 'discharged' patient's forwarding address on file traces back to a family that never received her.
The handwriting on at least six consent forms matches Dr. Moorhouse's private correspondence, not the patients' own signatures.
A section of ground behind the ward's rear wall has been disturbed and resealed at least four times in the past two years.
Dr. Wycliffe's gambling debts were paid in full the same month he began countersigning Dr. Moorhouse's discharge papers.

knows: Moorhouse has now admitted outright that patients died under his procedures and are buried behind the ward.
hides: She hides how frightened she still is, needing to seem unshakeable so Moorhouse doesn't sense he can still frighten her into silence.
believes_falsely: She had hoped a confession, once heard, would be enough on its own to end it — and is only now realizing it won't be.

knows: His tip to the board triggered the inspector's letter, but it only asks about 'irregularities,' not the deaths he knows are real.
hides: That he softened his report to the board out of lingering self-preservation, naming numbers but not bodies.
believes_falsely: He believes naming 'irregularities' rather than deaths might still let him negotiate his own immunity when the audit comes.

knows: The inspector's letter names only irregularities, which he can survive, not murder, which he could not.
hides: His plan to reshape the coming audit's narrative before anyone connects the ledger to the ground behind the wall.
believes_falsely: He believes his reputation will let him outlast this the way it has outlasted every quiet death before it.
